text: This is a video of a patient that presented to the hospital with chief complaint of upper GI bleed. She had a CT scan of her abdomen and pelvis which demonstrated a radiopaque object in the first part of the duodenum. She had an AGD performed and there was a lot of inflammation on the first part of the duodenum on the anterior wall and posterior wall.
The surgeon performs a duodenotomy, guided by laparoscopic ultrasound, to locate the radiopaque object in the duodenum.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic ultrasound, revealing a radiopaque structure within the duodenum. The TilePro system is not functioning, complicating the visualization for the surgeon at the console.

The surgeon inserts a Maryland dissector into the first part of the anterior wall of the duodenum, attempting to locate the radiopaque object amidst significant edema. Two laparoscopic instruments are used for tactile feedback, and a laparoscopic ultrasound is repeated to precisely identify the object's location, while the CT scan shows the duodenum is collapsed.

The surgeon extends the duodenotomy transversely in the first part of the duodenum, opening the area injected with ICG the previous day. Despite the extension, the exact location of the radiopaque object remains difficult to pinpoint.

The surgeon removes the falciform ligament, planning to use it later as a flap to cover the repair. An ultrasound is repeated, revealing the radiopaque object located on the posterior wall of the duodenum.

Using laparoscopic ultrasound, the surgeon identifies the radiopaque foreign body, a chicken bone, on the posterior wall of the duodenum. An incision is made on the mucosa of the posterior wall to access the foreign body.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to close the mucosal defect on the posterior wall. A 2-0 silk suture is placed in the middle to align the duodenum for closure. The closure technique involves a running 3-0 V-lock suture, differing from the typical interrupted silk used in Heineken-Michels pyloroplasty.

The surgeon completes the third layer of suturing using silk sutures to secure the falciform flap. The sutures make it difficult to pass the endoscope, necessitating the removal of some sutures to successfully perform endoscopy and complete the procedure.
